approaches.name: Brute Force O(n! * n)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of vertices to explore every possible Hamiltonian path order, compute the total distance for each permutation, and track the minimum. Use this for small n (e.g., n <= 10) due to factorial growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Shortest Hamiltonian Path
ladders.desc: Find the shortest Hamiltonian path visiting all vertices exactly once using brute force permutation generation.
ladders.explanation: The Hamiltonian path problem requires visiting every vertex in a graph exactly once. The brute force approach explores all possible orderings of vertices to find the path with minimum total distance. This works because a Hamiltonian path is defined by the sequence of vertices visited—by generating all n! permutations, we guarantee examining every possible path. The intuition is exhaustive search: since we can't know which ordering yields the shortest path without checking, we systematically enumerate all permutations. For each permutation, we compute the total distance by summing edge weights between consecutive vertices (if edges exist; otherwise, we might treat as infinite distance or skip invalid paths). We track the minimum distance found. This approach is only feasible for small n (typically n ≤ 10) due to factorial time complexity. Step-by-step: 1. Generate all permutations of vertices [0, 1, ..., n-1]. 2. For each permutation, initialize total_distance = 0. 3. For i from 0 to n-2, add distance from permutation[i] to permutation[i+1] (from adjacency matrix). If any edge is missing (infinite weight), skip this permutation. 4. Update min_distance if total_distance is smaller. 5. Return min_distance. Time: O(n! * n) for generating permutations (n! of them) and processing each (O(n) to compute distance). Space: O(n) for storing current permutation and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This uses the permutation generation from L1 as a key subroutine. Common mistakes: not handling missing edges (must skip invalid paths), using O(n²) distance computation per permutation (should be O(n)), or missing the base case of n=1. Real code pattern: Use itertools.permutations in Python or recursive backtracking.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generating All Permutations of Vertices
ladders.desc: Generate all possible orderings (permutations) of n vertices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial because the brute force approach relies on examining every sequence in which vertices can be visited. A permutation is an arrangement of all n distinct vertices in some order—there are n! such permutations. The intuition is that by systematically generating these, we ensure no potential Hamiltonian path is missed. We can generate permutations using recursion with backtracking: start with an empty list, recursively add each unused vertex until all are used, then record the complete permutation. Alternatively, use iterative methods like Heap's algorithm or library functions. Step-by-step recursive algorithm: 1. Base case: if current permutation length equals n, add a copy to results. 2. Otherwise, for each vertex not yet used: mark it as used, add to permutation, recurse, then backtrack (mark unused and remove). Time: O(n * n!) since we generate n! permutations and each takes O(n) to copy or process. Space: O(n) for recursion depth and current permutation. Connection to L0: This provides the list of vertex sequences that L0 iterates over to compute path distances. Common mistakes: not copying permutations before storing (leading to references to same list), inefficient generation (e.g., using O(n²) checks for used vertices), or missing permutations due to incorrect backtracking. Real code pattern: Use a 'used' boolean array and recursive function.

examples.trace: Start with empty perm=[], used=[F,F,F]. Level 1: try i=0: perm=[0], used[T,F,F]. Level 2: try i=1: perm=[0,1], used[T,T,F]. Level 3: try i=2: perm=[0,1,2], used[T,T,T] -> add [0,1,2]. Backtrack: remove 2, used[T,T,F]. No more at level 3, backtrack to level 2: remove 1, used[T,F,F]. Level 2 try i=2: perm=[0,2], used[T,F,T]. Level 3 try i=1: perm=[0,2,1], used[T,T,T] -> add [0,2,1]. Backtrack similarly. After exhausting i=0, try i=1 and i=2 similarly to get all 6 permutations.

Start perm=[], used=[F,F]. Try i=0: perm=[0], used[T,F]. Try i=1: perm=[0,1], used[T,T] -> add [0,1]. Backtrack: remove 1, used[T,F]. No more, backtrack: remove 0, used[F,F]. Try i=1: perm=[1], used[F,T]. Try i=0: perm=[1,0], used[T,T] -> add [1,0]. Done.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Computing Path Distance for a Given Vertex Order
ladders.desc: Calculate total distance of a Hamiltonian path given a specific vertex sequence.
ladders.explanation: This is the core logic that evaluates a candidate path. Given a permutation (ordered list of vertices) and a distance matrix, we sum the edge weights between consecutive vertices to get the total path distance. The intuition is straightforward: a path's length is the sum of its edges. For a sequence of vertices v0, v1, ..., v_{n-1}, the distance is dist[v0][v1] + dist[v1][v2] + ... + dist[v_{n-2}][v_{n-1}]. This assumes the graph is complete or we handle missing edges (e.g., by returning infinity if any edge weight is infinite). Step-by-step: 1. Initialize total = 0. 2. For i from 0 to n-2: total += dist[perm[i]][perm[i+1]]. 3. If any dist is INF (or very large), we can early return INF to indicate invalid path. 4. Return total. Time: O(n) for iterating through n-1 edges. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This function takes each permutation from L1 and computes its distance for comparison in L0. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (looping to n-1 instead of n-2), not handling single-vertex paths (n=1 should return 0), or incorrectly accessing distance matrix (ensure indices are within bounds). Real code pattern: Simple for-loop over the permutation list.

examples.trace: n=3. i=0: dist[2][0]=4, total=4. i=1: dist[0][1]=INF, so return INF (or total becomes INF).

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing Graph with Adjacency Matrix
ladders.desc: Store graph edge distances in a 2D matrix for efficient lookup.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency matrix is a square matrix where entry dist[i][j] represents the distance (or weight) from vertex i to vertex j. For the Hamiltonian path problem, this allows O(1) access to edge weights when computing path distances. The intuition is that we need quick queries for distances between any two vertices—a matrix provides constant-time indexing. If the graph is undirected, the matrix is symmetric (dist[i][j] = dist[j][i]); if directed, it may not be. Missing edges can be represented by a special value like INF (a very large number). Step-by-step: Given n vertices, create an n x n matrix initialized appropriately. For each edge (i,j) with weight w, set dist[i][j] = w. Typically, dist[i][i] = 0. Time to build: O(n²) if initializing all entries, or O(E) for setting edges. Space: O(n²). Connection to L2: This matrix is the 'dist' input used in L2 to compute path distances. Common mistakes: not handling zero-based vs one-based indexing, assuming symmetry incorrectly, or using inefficient representations like adjacency lists that require O(degree) lookup. Real code pattern: 2D list in Python, with INF = float('inf').

examples.trace: Create 3x3 matrix. Set dist[0][0]=0, dist[1][1]=0, dist[2][2]=0. Edge 0-1 weight 5: set dist[0][1]=5, dist[1][0]=5 (undirected). Edge 0-2 weight 3: set dist[0][2]=3, dist[2][0]=3. Edge 1-2 weight 2: set dist[1][2]=2, dist[2][1]=2. Fill remaining with 0 on diagonal.

Create 4x4, init all to INF, diagonal to 0. Edge 0-1: set dist[0][1]=2, dist[1][0]=2. Edge 1-2: set dist[1][2]=6, dist[2][1]=6. Edge 2-3: set dist[2][3]=8, dist[3][2]=8. Edge 0-3: set dist[0][3]=10, dist[3][0]=10. Others remain INF.

testCases.input: n=5, edges: all pairs with weight 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Array Indices with Loops
ladders.desc: Use loops to traverse sequences like permutations or matrix rows/columns.
ladders.explanation: Iteration is fundamental to processing collections of data. In the Hamiltonian path problem, we need to loop over vertices in a permutation to sum distances, or over permutations themselves. The intuition is repetitive action: perform an operation for each element in a list. A for-loop allows executing a block of code multiple times, typically with a counter or iterator. For example, to compute path distance: for i in range(len(perm)-1): total += dist[perm[i]][perm[i+1]]. This accesses consecutive pairs. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize loop variable (e.g., i=0). 2. Check condition (e.g., i < n-1). 3. Execute body (add distance). 4. Increment i. 5. Repeat until condition false. Time depends on loop length; here O(n). Space: O(1) for loop variable. Connection to L3: This operation is used to build or query the adjacency matrix (e.g., nested loops to initialize). Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., using range(n) instead of range(n-1)), modifying loop variable incorrectly, or infinite loops. Real code pattern: 'for i in range(n):' in Python.

examples.trace: Initialize total=0. i=0: arr[0]=5, total=5. i=1: arr[1]=3, total=8. i=2: arr[2]=7, total=15. Loop ends after i=2 since range(3) gives i=0,1,2.
n=3. Loop i from 0 to 1. i=0: pair=(perm[0],perm[1])=(2,0). i=1: pair=(perm[1],perm[2])=(0,1). Done.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Store and access elements in a list using positions (indices).
ladders.explanation: An array (or list) is a collection of items stored in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index (position). In programming, we use zero-based indexing: the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. This is foundational because the Hamiltonian path problem represents vertices as indices (e.g., 0 to n-1), and distances are stored in a 2D array (matrix). The intuition is organization: by assigning each vertex a number, we can easily refer to them and look up distances. For example, if vertices are [A,B,C], we might map A=0, B=1, C=2. Then dist[0][1] gives distance from A to B. Step-by-step: To access an element, use syntax like arr[i]. Ensure i is within bounds (0 ≤ i < length). Arrays support operations like reading, writing, and iterating. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n) for storage. Connection to L4: Arrays are the data structures we iterate over with loops. Common mistakes: using one-based indexing incorrectly, accessing out-of-bounds indices (causing errors), or confusing arrays with other data structures. Real code pattern: In Python, list = [10,20,30]; list[0] returns 10.

examples.trace: arr is a list with elements: index 0:5, index 1:8, index 2:3. So arr[1] returns the element at position 1, which is 8.
matrix is a list of lists. matrix[0] is [0,2]. Then matrix[0][1] accesses the second element of that inner list, which is 2.

approaches.name: DP with Bitmasking O(n^2 * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Use dynamic programming with a bitmask to represent visited vertices, storing the minimum cost to reach a subset ending at a specific vertex. Iterate over subsets to build solutions incrementally. This is the standard approach for medium-sized n (e.g., n <= 20).

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Hamiltonian Path with DP Bitmasking
ladders.desc: Find the minimum cost Hamiltonian path visiting all vertices exactly once using DP with bitmask representation.
ladders.explanation: The Hamiltonian path problem requires visiting every vertex exactly once. For n vertices, we need to track which vertices have been visited and the current endpoint. We use a bitmask (integer) where bit i is 1 if vertex i is visited. DP[mask][i] stores the minimum cost to visit vertices in mask ending at vertex i. We initialize DP[1<<i][i] = 0 for each starting vertex i. Then we iterate over all masks from smallest to largest. For each mask and endpoint i, we try extending the path by adding an unvisited vertex j. The transition is: DP[mask|(1<<j)][j] = min(DP[mask|(1<<j)][j], DP[mask][i] + cost[i][j]). After processing all masks, the answer is min(DP[(1<<n)-1][i]) for all i, where (1<<n)-1 is the full mask with all n bits set. This works because we build solutions incrementally: any Hamiltonian path ending at i can be formed by taking a path to some previous vertex j and adding edge (j,i). The bitmask ensures we don't revisit vertices. Time is O(n² * 2ⁿ) from nested loops over masks (2ⁿ), endpoints (n), and next vertices (n). Space is O(n * 2ⁿ) for the DP table. Common mistakes: not initializing properly, incorrect mask iteration order (must process smaller masks first), forgetting to handle asymmetric costs, and off-by-one errors in bit operations. This connects to L1 by using the DP transition as the key subroutine.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Transition with Bitmask Extension
ladders.desc: Extend a DP state by adding one unvisited vertex to the path.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core transition in the DP. Given a current state represented by a bitmask 'mask' and an endpoint vertex 'i', we extend the path by adding an unvisited vertex 'j'. The mask is an integer where bit k is 1 if vertex k is visited. First, check if vertex j is unvisited: (mask & (1<<j)) == 0. If so, compute the new mask: new_mask = mask | (1<<j). The cost to reach j via i is DP[mask][i] + cost[i][j]. Update DP[new_mask][j] = min(DP[new_mask][j], this_cost). This works because any path ending at j with vertices new_mask must come from a path ending at some i in mask (with j not in mask), plus the edge (i,j). The bitmask ensures we don't revisit vertices. The algorithm iterates over all j not in mask. Time per state is O(n) to check all j. Space is O(1) per transition. Common mistakes: incorrect bit checking (using ==0 vs !=0), not using min update (might overwrite better values), and forgetting that cost matrix might be asymmetric. This connects to L0 as the inner loop in the full solution, and to L2 as it relies on bitmask representation.

examples.trace: Vertices: 0,1,2. mask=0011 means vertices 0 and 1 visited (bits 0 and 1 set). i=0 is endpoint. Check j=0: (0011 & 0001)=0001 !=0, skip. j=1: (0011 & 0010)=0010 !=0, skip. j=2: (0011 & 0100)=0000 ==0, unvisited. new_mask=0011|0100=0111. cost[0][2]=2. new_cost=DP[0011][0]+2=5+2=7. Update DP[0111][2]=min(inf,7)=7.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Vertex Subsets
ladders.desc: Use bitmask integers to represent subsets of visited vertices.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is to represent a subset of vertices as an integer bitmask. For n vertices, use an n-bit integer where bit i (0-indexed) is 1 if vertex i is in the subset, else 0. This allows efficient set operations: check if vertex i is in mask: (mask & (1<<i)) != 0; add vertex i to mask: mask | (1<<i); remove vertex i: mask & ~(1<<i). In DP, mask represents the set of visited vertices. We iterate masks from 0 to (1<<n)-1, ensuring smaller subsets are processed before larger ones (since extending a path adds vertices). This works because there are 2ⁿ possible subsets, and bitmask allows O(1) set operations. The DP state DP[mask][i] means minimum cost to visit exactly the vertices in mask, ending at i. Time to iterate all masks is O(2ⁿ). Space for masks is O(2ⁿ) but combined with endpoints gives O(n*2ⁿ). Common mistakes: incorrect bit indexing (off-by-one), not using 0-indexing, misunderstanding bit operations (e.g., using ^ instead of | for union), and not handling large n where 2ⁿ is huge. This connects to L1 as it provides the mask representation for transitions, and to L3 as it builds on binary representation.

examples.trace: Vertices are 0,1,2. Bit positions: 0→bit0, 1→bit1, 2→bit2. Vertex 0 in subset: set bit0: 1<<0=0001. Vertex 2 in subset: set bit2: 1<<2=0100. Combine with OR: 0001|0100=0101 binary = 5 decimal. Check: mask & (1<<0)=0101&0001=0001≠0, yes contains 0; mask & (1<<1)=0101&0010=0000=0, not contains 1; mask & (1<<2)=0101&0100=0100≠0, yes contains 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over All Subsets Using Bitmasks
ladders.desc: Generate all subsets of n elements by iterating integers from 0 to 2ⁿ-1.
ladders.explanation: To solve the DP, we need to process all subsets of vertices. For n elements, there are 2ⁿ subsets. We represent each subset as an integer from 0 to 2ⁿ-1, where bit i corresponds to element i. Iterate mask from 0 to (1<<n)-1 inclusive. Each mask corresponds to a unique subset. For example, for n=3: mask=0 (000) empty set, 1 (001) {0}, 2 (010) {1}, 3 (011) {0,1}, up to 7 (111) {0,1,2}. This works because binary counting enumerates all combinations. In DP, we often iterate in increasing order of mask value, which ensures subsets are processed before their supersets (since adding bits increases value). Time is O(2ⁿ) to iterate. Space is O(1) per iteration. Common mistakes: off-by-one in loop range (use < vs <=), not using 1<<n correctly for large n (ensure integer type), and misunderstanding that mask order doesn't guarantee all subsets of size k come before all of size k+1 (but it's fine for DP if we check bits). This connects to L2 as it uses bitmask representation, and to L4 as it relies on bit operations.

examples.trace: Total subsets: 2²=4. Loop mask from 0 to (1<<2)-1=3. mask=0: binary 00, subset {}. mask=1: binary 01, subset {0}. mask=2: binary 10, subset {1}. mask=3: binary 11, subset {0,1}. Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bit Manipulation for Set Membership
ladders.desc: Check if a bit is set, set a bit, or clear a bit using bitwise operators.
ladders.explanation: Bit manipulation uses bitwise operators on integers. Key operations: 1) Check if bit i is set in mask: (mask & (1<<i)) != 0. Here, 1<<i creates a number with only bit i set (e.g., for i=2, 1<<2=4 binary 100). AND with mask yields 0 if bit i is 0, non-zero if 1. 2) Set bit i in mask: mask | (1<<i). OR turns bit i to 1. 3) Clear bit i: mask & ~(1<<i). ~ is bitwise NOT, flipping bits. For example, ~(1<<2) = ...11111011, AND clears bit 2. These are O(1) operations. In DP, we use check to see if a vertex is visited, set to add a vertex. Works because integers are stored in binary. Common mistakes: using ==1 instead of !=0 for check (bit might be in other positions), incorrect operator precedence (use parentheses), and not using 0-indexing. This connects to L3 as it enables subset iteration, and to L5 as it builds on binary numbers.

examples.output: check false, set to 7, clear to 5
examples.trace: mask=5 decimal = 0101 binary. Check bit1: 1<<1=2 (0010). mask & 0010 = 0101&0010=0000=0, so false (bit not set). Set bit1: mask | 0010 = 0101|0010=0111=7. Clear bit0: 1<<0=1 (0001), ~0001=1110, mask & 1110 = 0101&1110=0100=4, but since bit0 was set, clearing changes mask to 4. For clear bit1: bit1 wasn't set, so mask stays 5.

examples.output: check false, set to 10, clear to 10
examples.trace: mask=10 decimal = 1010 binary. Check bit3: 1<<3=8 (1000). mask & 1000 = 1010&1000=1000=8≠0, so true (bit set). Set bit3: already set, mask remains 10. Clear bit3: ~1000=0111, mask & 0111 = 1010&0111=0010=2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Numbers and Bitwise Operators
ladders.desc: Understand binary representation and basic bitwise operations.
ladders.explanation: Binary is base-2 numbering using digits 0 and 1. Each digit is a bit. For example, decimal 5 is binary 101 (1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1). Bitwise operators work on bits: AND (&) outputs 1 if both bits are 1; OR (|) outputs 1 if at least one bit is 1; XOR (^) outputs 1 if bits differ; NOT (~) flips bits; left shift (<<) shifts bits left, equivalent to multiplying by 2; right shift (>>) shifts right, dividing by 2. For example, 3 & 5: 3=011, 5=101, 011&101=001=1. 3|5=111=7. 3<<1=6 (011→110). These are fast hardware operations. In programming, integers are stored in binary, so these manipulate the underlying bits. This foundation is crucial for bitmasking because we use bits to represent set membership. Common mistakes: confusing bitwise AND with logical AND, not understanding two's complement for negative numbers, and misusing shift operators. This connects to L4 as it provides the basic operations for bit manipulation.
